meeting.title: Intro with Sarah from Ramp
meeting.description: Intro call to discuss Ramp card program and spend controls.

company.name: Ramp
company.summary: Corporate card and spend management platform. Automates expense policy, bill pay, and accounting workflows for finance teams.

briefing.summary60s: Sarah Chen is an AE at Ramp, a finance automation platform focused on corporate cards, spend controls, and bill pay. Lead with total spend visibility and close with the accounting automations your team already notes in Notion.
briefing.suggestedOpeningLine: Sarah, thanks for making time. I want to skip the overview and use the 30 minutes to pressure-test whether Ramp fits our QuickBooks setup and policy library. Cool if I start with how our controller is thinking about this?
questionsToAsk: How does Ramp's QuickBooks sync handle class and location tags on card transactions?
What does the policy migration look like when we come off an existing corporate card program?
Which customers closest to our size cut receipt-chasing time with Ramp, and by how much?
What is the realistic timeline from signed contract to cards issued and first statement closed?
If we want Ramp Bill Pay next quarter, what does pricing look like at our AP volume?
likelyPainPoints: Migration friction from re-keying vendor records and policy rules.
QuickBooks sync edge cases around class, location, and custom fields.
Change management for employees used to the current card workflow.
risks: Procurement and legal review could push a close into Q4 if the MSA is not standard.
Sarah may keep Ramp Plus and Bill Pay pricing vague until late-stage.
briefing.followUpEmail: Hi Sarah, thanks for the intro call. Quick recap: spend visibility sounds strong, QuickBooks sync needs a technical check, and we need written pricing for Ramp Plus and Bill Pay before taking this to the controller.
